sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Stain to thy countrymen, thou hear'st thy doom!
Be packing, therefore, thou that wast a knight:
Henceforth we banish thee, on pain of death.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
And now, my lord protector, view the letter
Sent from our uncle Duke of Burgundy.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
What means his grace, that he hath changed his style?
No more but, plain and bluntly, 'To the king!'
Hath he forgot he is his sovereign?
Or doth this churlish superscription
Pretend some alteration in good will?
What's here?

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
'I have, upon especial cause,
Moved with compassion of my country's wreck,
Together with the pitiful complaints
Of such as your oppression feeds upon,
Forsaken your pernicious faction
And join'd with Charles, the rightful King of France.'
O monstrous treachery! can this be so,
That in alliance, amity and oaths,
There should be found such false dissembling guile?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: What! doth my uncle Burgundy revolt?

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text: He doth, my lord, and is become your foe.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text: He doth, my lord, and is become your foe.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: Is that the worst this letter doth contain?

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text: It is the worst, and all, my lord, he writes.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Why, then, Lord Talbot there shall talk with him
And give him chastisement for this abuse.
How say you, my lord? are you not content?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
Content, my liege! yes, but that I am prevented,
I should have begg'd I might have been employ'd.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Then gather strength and march unto him straight:
Let him perceive how ill we brook his treason
And what offence it is to flout his friends.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Then gather strength and march unto him straight:
Let him perceive how ill we brook his treason
And what offence it is to flout his friends.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
I go, my lord, in heart desiring still
You may behold confusion of your foes.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
I go, my lord, in heart desiring still

Exit
Enter VERNON and BASSET

sentences.speaker: VERNON
sentences.text: Grant me the combat, gracious sovereign.

sentences.speaker: BASSET
sentences.text: And me, my lord, grant me the combat too.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: This is my servant: hear him, noble prince.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: This is my servant: hear him, noble prince.

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text: And this is mine: sweet Henry, favour him.

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text: And this is mine: sweet Henry, favour him.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Be patient, lords; and give them leave to speak.
Say, gentlemen, what makes you thus exclaim?
And wherefore crave you combat? or with whom?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Be patient, lords; and give them leave to speak.
Say, gentlemen, what makes you thus exclaim?
And wherefore crave you combat? or with whom?

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Be patient, lords; and give them leave to speak.
Say, gentlemen, what makes you thus exclaim?
And wherefore crave you combat? or with whom?

sentences.speaker: VERNON
sentences.text: With him, my lord; for he hath done me wrong.

sentences.speaker: BASSET
sentences.text: And I with him; for he hath done me wrong.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
What is that wrong whereof you both complain?
First let me know, and then I'll answer you.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
What is that wrong whereof you both complain?
First let me know, and then I'll answer you.

sentences.speaker: BASSET
sentences.text:
Crossing the sea from England into France,
This fellow here, with envious carping tongue,
Upbraided me about the rose I wear;
Saying, the sanguine colour of the leaves
Did represent my master's blushing cheeks,
When stubbornly he did repugn the truth
About a certain question in the law
Argued betwixt the Duke of York and him;
With other vile and ignominious terms:
In confutation of which rude reproach
And in defence of my lord's worthiness,
I crave the benefit of law of arms.

sentences.speaker: VERNON
sentences.text:
And that is my petition, noble lord:
For though he seem with forged quaint conceit
To set a gloss upon his bold intent,
Yet know, my lord, I was provoked by him;
And he first took exceptions at this badge,
Pronouncing that the paleness of this flower
Bewray'd the faintness of my master's heart.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Will not this malice, Somerset, be left?

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text:
Your private grudge, my Lord of York, will out,
Though ne'er so cunningly you smother it.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Good Lord, what madness rules in brainsick men,
When for so slight and frivolous a cause
Such factious emulations shall arise!
Good cousins both, of York and Somerset,
Quiet yourselves, I pray, and be at peace.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:

Good cousins both, of York and Somerset,

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Let this dissension first be tried by fight,
And then your highness shall command a peace.

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text:
The quarrel toucheth none but us alone;
Betwixt ourselves let us decide it then.

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text:
The quarrel toucheth none but us alone;
Betwixt ourselves let us decide it then.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: There is my pledge; accept it, Somerset.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: There is my pledge; accept it, Somerset.

sentences.speaker: VERNON
sentences.text: Nay, let it rest where it began at first.

sentences.speaker: BASSET
sentences.text: Confirm it so, mine honourable lord.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
Confirm it so! Confounded be your strife!
And perish ye, with your audacious prate!
Presumptuous vassals, are you not ashamed
With this immodest clamorous outrage
To trouble and disturb the king and us?
And you, my lords, methinks you do not well
To bear with their perverse objections;
Much less to take occasion from their mouths
To raise a mutiny betwixt yourselves:
Let me persuade you take a better course.

sentences.speaker: EXETER
sentences.text: It grieves his highness: good my lords, be friends.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Come hither, you that would be combatants:
Henceforth I charge you, as you love our favour,
Quite to forget this quarrel and the cause.
And you, my lords, remember where we are,
In France, amongst a fickle wavering nation:
If they perceive dissension in our looks
And that within ourselves we disagree,
How will their grudging stomachs be provoked
To wilful disobedience, and rebel!
Beside, what infamy will there arise,
When foreign princes shall be certified
That for a toy, a thing of no regard,
King Henry's peers and chief nobility
Destroy'd themselves, and lost the realm of France!
O, think upon the conquest of my father,
My tender years, and let us not forego
That for a trifle that was bought with blood
Let me be umpire in this doubtful strife.
I see no reason, if I wear this rose,

How will their grudging stomachs be provoked
To wilful disobedience, and rebel!
Beside, what infamy will there arise,
When foreign princes shall be certified
That for a toy, a thing of no regard,
King Henry's peers and chief nobility
Destroy'd themselves, and lost the realm of France!
O, think upon the conquest of my father,
My tender years, and let us not forego
That for a trifle that was bought with blood
Let me be umpire in this doubtful strife.
I see no reason, if I wear this rose,

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Come hither, you that would be combatants:
Henceforth I charge you, as you love our favour,
Quite to forget this quarrel and the cause.
And you, my lords, remember where we are,
In France, amongst a fickle wavering nation:

And that within ourselves we disagree,

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
That any one should therefore be suspicious
I more incline to Somerset than York:
Both are my kinsmen, and I love them both:
As well they may upbraid me with my crown,
Because, forsooth, the king of Scots is crown'd.
But your discretions better can persuade
Than I am able to instruct or teach:
And therefore, as we hither came in peace,
So let us still continue peace and love.
Cousin of York, we institute your grace
To be our regent in these parts of France:
And, good my Lord of Somerset, unite
Your troops of horsemen with his bands of foot;
And, like true subjects, sons of your progenitors,
Go cheerfully together and digest.
Your angry choler on your enemies.
Ourself, my lord protector and the rest
After some respite will return to Calais;
From thence to England; where I hope ere long
To be presented, by your victories,
With Charles, Alencon and that traitorous rout.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
My Lord of York, I promise you, the king
Prettily, methought, did play the orator.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
And so he did; but yet I like it not,
In that he wears the badge of Somerset.

sentences.speaker: YORK

I dare presume, sweet prince, he thought no harm.

Exeunt all but EXETER

sentences.speaker: EXETER
sentences.text:
Well didst thou, Richard, to suppress thy voice;
For, had the passions of thy heart burst out,
I fear we should have seen decipher'd there
More rancorous spite, more furious raging broils,
Than yet can be imagined or supposed.
But howsoe'er, no simple man that sees
This jarring discord of nobility,
This shouldering of each other in the court,
This factious bandying of their favourites,
But that it doth presage some ill event.
'Tis much when sceptres are in children's hands;
But more when envy breeds unkind division;
There comes the rain, there begins confusion.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
Go to the gates of Bourdeaux, trumpeter:
Summon their general unto the wall.
Trumpet sounds. Enter General and others, aloft
Trumpet sounds. Enter General and others, aloft

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
TALBOT
TALBOT
TALBOT
TALBOT
TALBOT
GENERAL
sentences.text:
Thou ominous and fearful owl of death,
Our nation's terror and their bloody scourge!
The period of thy tyranny approacheth.
On us thou canst not enter but by death;
For, I protest, we are well fortified
And strong enough to issue out and fight:
If thou retire, the Dauphin, well appointed,
Stands with the snares of war to tangle thee:
On either hand thee there are squadrons pitch'd,
To wall thee from the liberty of flight;
And no way canst thou turn thee for redress,
But death doth front thee with apparent spoil
And pale destruction meets thee in the face.
Ten thousand French have ta'en the sacrament
To rive their dangerous artillery
Upon no Christian soul but English Talbot.
Lo, there thou stand'st, a breathing valiant man,
Of an invincible unconquer'd spirit!
This is the latest glory of thy praise
That I, thy enemy, due thee withal;
For ere the glass, that now begins to run,
Finish the process of his sandy hour,
These eyes, that see thee now well coloured,
Shall see thee wither'd, bloody, pale and dead.

And pale destruction meets thee in the face.
Ten thousand French have ta'en the sacrament
To rive their dangerous artillery
Upon no Christian soul but English Talbot.
Lo, there thou stand'st, a breathing valiant man,
Of an invincible unconquer'd spirit!
This is the latest glory of thy praise
That I, thy enemy, due thee withal;
For ere the glass, that now begins to run,
Finish the process of his sandy hour,
These eyes, that see thee now well coloured,
Shall see thee wither'd, bloody, pale and dead.

sentences.speaker: GENERAL
sentences.text:
Thou ominous and fearful owl of death,
Our nation's terror and their bloody scourge!
The period of thy tyranny approacheth.
On us thou canst not enter but by death;
For, I protest, we are well fortified
And strong enough to issue out and fight:
If thou retire, the Dauphin, well appointed,
Stands with the snares of war to tangle thee:
On either hand thee there are squadrons pitch'd,
To wall thee from the liberty of flight;
And no way canst thou turn thee for redress,
But death doth front thee with apparent spoil
And pale destruction meets thee in the face.
Ten thousand French have ta'en the sacrament
To rive their dangerous artillery
Upon no Christian soul but English Talbot.
Lo, there thou stand'st, a breathing valiant man,
Of an invincible unconquer'd spirit!
This is the latest glory of thy praise
That I, thy enemy, due thee withal;
For ere the glass, that now begins to run,
Finish the process of his sandy hour,
These eyes, that see thee now well coloured,
Shall see thee wither'd, bloody, pale and dead.
Drum afar off

sentences.speaker: GENERAL
GENERAL
GENERAL
GENERAL

sentences.text: Exeunt General, & c

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
He fables not; I hear the enemy:
Out, some light horsemen, and peruse their wings.
O, negligent and heedless discipline!
How are we park'd and bounded in a pale,
A little herd of England's timorous deer,
Mazed with a yelping kennel of French curs!
If we be English deer, be then in blood;
Not rascal-like, to fall down with a pinch,
But rather, moody-mad and desperate stags ,
Turn on the bloody hounds with heads of steel
And make the cowards stand aloof at bay:
Sell every man his life as dear as mine,
And they shall find dear deer of us, my friends.
God and Saint George, Talbot and England's right,
Prosper our colours in this dangerous fight!

Enter a Messenger that meets YORK. Enter YORK with trumpet and many Soldiers
Enter a Messenger that meets YORK. Enter YORK with trumpet and many Soldiers

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Are not the speedy scouts return'd again,
That dogg'd the mighty army of the Dauphin?

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
They are return'd, my lord, and give it out
That he is march'd to Bourdeaux with his power,
To fight with Talbot: as he march'd along,
By your espials were discovered
Two mightier troops than that the Dauphin led,
Which join'd with him and made their march for
Bourdeaux.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
A plague upon that villain Somerset,
That thus delays my promised supply
Of horsemen, that were levied for this siege!
Renowned Talbot doth expect my aid,
And I am lowted by a traitor villain
And cannot help the noble chevalier:
God comfort him in this necessity!
If he miscarry, farewell wars in France.

sentences.speaker: LUCY
sentences.text:
Thou princely leader of our English strength,
Never so needful on the earth of France,
Spur to the rescue of the noble Talbot,
Who now is girdled with a waist of iron
And hemm'd about with grim destruction:
To Bourdeaux, warlike duke! to Bourdeaux, York!
Else, farewell Talbot, France, and England's honour.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
O God, that Somerset, who in proud heart
Doth stop my cornets, were in Talbot's place!
So should we save a valiant gentleman
By forfeiting a traitor and a coward.
Mad ire and wrathful fury makes me weep,
That thus we die, while remiss traitors sleep.

sentences.speaker: YORK
LUCY
LUCY
YORK
sentences.text:
Alas, what joy shall noble Talbot have
To bid his young son welcome to his grave?
Away! vexation almost stops my breath,
That sunder'd friends greet in the hour of death.
Lucy, farewell; no more my fortune can,
But curse the cause I cannot aid the man.
Maine, Blois, Poictiers, and Tours, are won away,
'Long all of Somerset and his delay.

sentences.speaker: LUCY
sentences.text:
Thus, while the vulture of sedition
Feeds in the bosom of such great commanders,
Sleeping neglection doth betray to loss
The conquest of our scarce cold conqueror,
That ever living man of memory,
Henry the Fifth: whiles they each other cross,
Lives, honours, lands and all hurry to loss.

sentences.speaker: LUCY

Exit
Enter SOMERSET, with his army; a Captain of TALBOT's with him

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text:
It is too late; I cannot send them now:
This expedition was by York and Talbot
Too rashly plotted: all our general force
Might with a sally of the very town
Be buckled with: the over-daring Talbot
Hath sullied all his gloss of former honour
By this unheedful, desperate, wild adventure:
York set him on to fight and die in shame,
That, Talbot dead, great York might bear the name.

sentences.speaker: CAPTAIN
sentences.text:
Here is Sir William Lucy, who with me
Set from our o'ermatch'd forces forth for aid.

sentences.speaker: CAPTAIN
sentences.text:
Here is Sir William Lucy, who with me
Set from our o'ermatch'd forces forth for aid.

sentences.speaker: CAPTAIN
sentences.text:
Here is Sir William Lucy, who with me
Set from our o'ermatch'd forces forth for aid.

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text: How now, Sir William! whither were you sent?

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text: How now, Sir William! whither were you sent?

sentences.speaker: LUCY
sentences.text:
Whither, my lord? from bought and sold Lord Talbot;
Who, ring'd about with bold adversity,
Cries out for noble York and Somerset,
To beat assailing death from his weak legions:
And whiles the honourable captain there
Drops bloody sweat from his war-wearied limbs,
And, in advantage lingering, looks for rescue,
You, his false hopes, the trust of England's honour,
Keep off aloof with worthless emulation.
Let not your private discord keep away
The levied succors that should lend him aid,
While he, renowned noble gentleman,
Yields up his life unto a world of odds:
Orleans the Bastard, Charles, Burgundy,
Alencon, Reignier, compass him about,
And Talbot perisheth by your default.

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text: York set him on; York should have sent him aid.

sentences.speaker: LUCY
SOMERSET
SOMERSET
SOMERSET
LUCY
sentences.text:
The fraud of England, not the force of France,
Hath now entrapp'd the noble-minded Talbot:
Never to England shall he bear his life;
But dies, betray'd to fortune by your strife.

sentences.speaker: SOMERSET
sentences.text:
Come, go; I will dispatch the horsemen straight:
Within six hours they will be at his aid.

sentences.speaker: LUCY
LUCY
SOMERSET
sentences.text: If he be dead, brave Talbot, then adieu!

sentences.speaker: LUCY
sentences.text: His fame lives in the world, his shame in you.
Exeunt
Enter TALBOT and JOHN his son

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
O young John Talbot! I did send for thee
To tutor thee in stratagems of war,
That Talbot's name might be in thee revived
When sapless age and weak unable limbs
Should bring thy father to his drooping c hair.
But, O malignant and ill-boding stars!
Now thou art come unto a feast of death,
A terrible and unavoided danger:
Therefore, dear boy, mount on my swiftest horse;
And I'll direct thee how thou shalt escape
By sudden flight: come, dally not, be gone.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
O young John Talbot! I did send for thee
To tutor thee in stratagems of war,
That Talbot's name might be in thee revived
When sapless age and weak unable limbs
Should bring thy father to his drooping c hair.
But, O malignant and ill-boding stars!
Now thou art come unto a feast of death,
A terrible and unavoided danger:
Therefore, dear boy, mount on my swiftest horse;
And I'll direct thee how thou shalt escape
By sudden flight: come, dally not, be gone.

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text:
Is my name Talbot? and am I your son?
And shall I fly? O if you love my mother,
Dishonour not her honourable name,
To make a bastard and a slave of me!
The world will say, he is not Talbot's blood,
That basely fled when noble Talbot stood.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text: Fly, to revenge my death, if I be slain.

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text: He that flies so will ne'er return again.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text: If we both stay, we both are sure to die.

sentences.text: Shall all thy mother's hopes lie in one tomb?

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text: Ay, rather than I'll shame my mother's womb.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text: Upon my blessing, I command thee go.

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text: To fight I will, but not to fly the foe.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text: Part of thy father may be saved in thee.

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text: No part of him but will be shame in me.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text: Thou never hadst renown, nor canst not lose it.

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text: Yes, your renowned name: shall flight abuse it?

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text: Yes, your renowned name: shall flight abuse it?

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text: Thy father's charge shall clear thee from that stain.

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text:
You cannot witness for me, being slain.
If death be so apparent, then both fly.

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text:
You cannot witness for me, being slain.
If death be so apparent, then both fly.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
And leave my followers here to fight and die?
My age was never tainted with such shame.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
And leave my followers here to fight and die?
My age was never tainted with such shame.

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text:
And shall my youth be guilty of such blame?
No more can I be sever'd from your side,
Than can yourself yourself in twain divide:
Stay, go, do what you will, the like do I;
For live I will not, if my father die.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
Then here I take my leave of thee, fair son,
Born to eclipse thy life this afternoon.
Come, side by side together live and die.
And soul with soul from France to heaven fly.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
Saint George and victory! fight, soldiers, fight.
The regent hath with Talbot broke his word
And left us to the rage of France his sword.
Where is John Talbot? Pause, and take thy breath;
I gave thee life and rescued thee from death.

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text:
O, twice my father, twice am I thy son!
The life thou gavest me first was lost and done,
Till with thy warlike sword, despite of late,
To my determined time thou gavest new date.

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text:
O, twice my father, twice am I thy son!
The life thou gavest me first was lost and done,
Till with thy warlike sword, despite of late,
To my determined time thou gavest new date.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
When from the Dauphin's crest thy sword struck fire,
It warm'd thy father's heart with proud desire
Of bold-faced victory. Then leaden age,
Quicken'd with youthful spleen and warlike rage,
Beat down Alencon, Orleans, Burgundy,
And from the pride of Gallia rescued thee.
The ireful bastard Orleans, that drew blood
From thee, my boy, and had the maidenhood
Of thy first fight, I soon encountered,
And interchanging blows I quickly shed
Some of his bastard blood; and in disgrace
Bespoke him thus; 'Contaminated, base
And misbegotten blood I spill of thine,
Mean and right poor, for that pure blood of mine
Which thou didst force from Talbot, my brave boy:'
Here, purposing the Bastard to destroy,
Came in strong rescue. Speak, thy father's care,
Art thou not weary, John? how dost thou fare?
Wilt thou yet leave the battle, boy, and fly,
Now thou art seal'd the son of chivalry?
Fly, to revenge my death when I am dead:
The help of one stands me in little stead.
O, too much folly is it, well I wot,
To hazard all our lives in one small boat!
If I to-day die not with Frenchmen's rage,
To-morrow I shall die with mickle age:
By me they nothing gain an if I stay;
'Tis but the shortening of my life one day:
In thee thy mother dies, our household's name,
My death's revenge, thy youth, and England's fame:
All these and more we hazard by thy stay;
All these are saved if thou wilt fly away.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
When from the Dauphin's crest thy sword struck fire,
It warm'd thy father's heart with proud desire
Of bold-faced victory. Then leaden age,
Quicken'd with youthful spleen and warlike rage,
Beat down Alencon, Orleans, Burgundy,
And from the pride of Gallia rescued thee.
The ireful bastard Orleans, that drew blood
From thee, my boy, and had the maidenhood
Of thy first fight, I soon encountered,
And interchanging blows I quickly shed
Some of his bastard blood; and in disgrace
Bespoke him thus; 'Contaminated, base
And misbegotten blood I spill of thine,
Mean and right poor, for that pure blood of mine
Which thou didst force from Talbot, my brave boy:'
Here, purposing the Bastard to destroy,
Came in strong rescue. Speak, thy father's care,
Art thou not weary, John? how dost thou fare?
Wilt thou yet leave the battle, boy, and fly,
Now thou art seal'd the son of chivalry?
Fly, to revenge my death when I am dead:
The help of one stands me in little stead.
O, too much folly is it, well I wot,
To hazard all our lives in one small boat!
If I to-day die not with Frenchmen's rage,
To-morrow I shall die with mickle age:
By me they nothing gain an if I stay;
'Tis but the shortening of my life one day:
In thee thy mother dies, our household's name,
My death's revenge, thy youth, and England's fame:
All these and more we hazard by thy stay;
All these are saved if thou wilt fly away.

sentences.speaker: JOHN TALBOT
sentences.text:
The sword of Orleans hath not made me smart;
These words of yours draw life-blood from my heart:
On that advantage, bought with such a shame,
To save a paltry life and slay bright fame,
Before young Talbot from old Talbot fly,
The coward horse that bears me fail and die!
And like me to the peasant boys of France,
To be shame's scorn and subject of mischance!
Surely, by all the glory you have won,
An if I fly, I am not Talbot's son:
Then talk no more of flight, it is no boot;
If son to Talbot, die at Talbot's foot.

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
Then follow thou thy desperate sire of Crete,
Thou Icarus; thy life to me is sweet:
If thou wilt fight, fight by thy father's side;
And, commendable proved, let's die in pride.

Alarum: excursions. Enter TALBOT led by a Servant
Alarum: excursions. Enter TALBOT led by a Servant
Alarum: excursions. Enter TALBOT led by a Servant

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
Where is my other life? mine own is gone;
O, where's young Talbot? where is valiant John?
Triumphant death, smear'd with captivity,
Young Talbot's valour makes me smile at thee:
When he perceived me shrink and on my knee,
His bloody sword he brandish'd over me,
And, like a hungry lion, did commence
Rough deeds of rage and stern impatience;
But when my angry guardant stood alone,
Tendering my ruin and assail'd of none,
Dizzy-eyed fury and great rage of heart
Suddenly made him from my side to start
Into the clustering battle of the French;
And in that sea of blood my boy did drench
His over-mounting spirit, and there died,
My Icarus, my blossom, in his pride.

sentences.speaker: SERVANT
sentences.text: O, my dear lord, lo, where your son is borne!
Enter Soldiers, with the body of JOHN TALBOT

sentences.speaker: TALBOT
sentences.text:
Thou antic death, which laugh'st us here to scorn,
Anon, from thy insulting tyranny,
Coupled in bonds of perpetuity,
Two Talbots, winged through the lither sky,
In thy despite shall 'scape mortality.
O, thou, whose wounds become hard-favour'd death,
Speak to thy father ere thou yield thy breath!
Brave death by speaking, whether he will or no;
Imagine him a Frenchman and thy foe.
Poor boy! he smiles, methinks, as who should say,
Had death been French, then death had died to-day.
Come, come and lay him in his father's arms:
My spirit can no longer bear these harms.
Soldiers, adieu! I have what I would have,
Now my old arms are young John Talbot's grave.

Enter CHARLES, ALENCON, BURGUNDY, BASTARD OF ORLEANS, JOAN LA PUCELLE, and forces

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
Had York and Somerset brought rescue in,
We should have found a bloody day of this.

sentences.speaker: BASTARD OF ORLEANS
sentences.text:
How the young whelp of Talbot's, raging-wood,
Did flesh his puny sword in Frenchmen's blood!

sentences.speaker: BASTARD OF ORLEANS
sentences.text:
How the young whelp of Talbot's, raging-wood,
Did flesh his puny sword in Frenchmen's blood!

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
Once I encounter'd him, and thus I said:
'Thou maiden youth, be vanquish'd by a maid:'
But, with a proud majestical high scorn,
He answer'd thus: 'Young Talbot was not born
To be the pillage of a giglot wench:'
So, rushing in the bowels of the French,
He left me proudly, as unworthy fight.

sentences.speaker: BURGUNDY
BURGUNDY
BASTARD OF ORLEANS
sentences.text:
Hew them to pieces, hack their bones asunder
Whose life was England's glory, Gallia's wonder.

sentences.speaker: BASTARD OF ORLEANS
sentences.text:
Hew them to pieces, hack their bones asunder
Whose life was England's glory, Gallia's wonder.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
O, no, forbear! for that which we have fled
During the life, let us not wrong it dead.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
O, no, forbear! for that which we have fled
During the life, let us not wrong it dead.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: LUCY
sentences.text:
Herald, conduct me to the Dauphin's tent,
To know who hath obtained the glory of the day.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text: On what submissive message art thou sent?

sentences.speaker: LUCY
sentences.text:
Submission, Dauphin! 'tis a mere French word;
We English warriors wot not what it means.
I come to know what prisoners thou hast ta'en
And to survey the bodies of the dead.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
For prisoners ask'st thou? hell our prison is.
But tell me whom thou seek'st.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
For prisoners ask'st thou? hell our prison is.
But tell me whom thou seek'st.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
For prisoners ask'st thou? hell our prison is.
But tell me whom thou seek'st.

sentences.speaker: LUCY
sentences.text:
But where's the great Alcides of the field,
Valiant Lord Talbot, Earl of Shrewsbury,
Created, for his rare success in arms,
Great Earl of Washford, Waterford and Valence;
Lord Talbot of Goodrig and Urchinfield,
Lord Strange of Blackmere, Lord Verdun of Alton,
Lord Cromwell of Wingfield, Lord Furnival of Sheffield,
The thrice-victorious Lord of Falconbridge;
Knight of the noble order of Saint George,
Worthy Saint Michael and the Golden Fleece;
Great marshal to Henry the Sixth
Of all his wars within the realm of France?

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
Here is a silly stately style indeed!
The Turk, that two and fifty kingdoms hath,
Writes not so tedious a style as this.
Him that thou magnifiest with all these titles
Stinking and fly-blown lies here at our feet.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE

sentences.speaker: LUCY
sentences.text:
Is Talbot slain, the Frenchmen's only scourge,
Your kingdom's terror and black Nemesis?
O, were mine eyeballs into bullets turn'd,
That I in rage might shoot them at your faces!
O, that I could but call these dead to life!
It were enough to fright the realm of France:
Were but his picture left amongst you here,
It would amaze the proudest of you all.
Give me their bodies, that I may bear them hence
And give them burial as beseems their worth.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
I think this upstart is old Talbot's ghost,
He speaks with such a proud commanding spirit.
For God's sake let him have 'em; to keep them here,
They would but stink, and putrefy the air.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text: Go, take their bodies hence.

sentences.speaker: LUCY
sentences.text:
I'll bear them hence; but from their ashes shall be
rear'd
A phoenix that shall make all France afeard.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
So we be rid of them, do with 'em what thou wilt.
And now to Paris, in this conquering vein:
All will be ours, now bloody Talbot's slain.

Sennet. Enter KING HENRY VI, GLOUCESTER, and EXETER
Sennet. Enter KING HENRY VI, GLOUCESTER, and EXETER

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Have you perused the letters from the pope,
The emperor and the Earl of Armagnac?

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
I have, my lord: and their intent is this:
They humbly sue unto your excellence
To have a godly peace concluded of
Between the realms of England and of France.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text: How doth your grace affect their motion?

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
Well, my good lord; and as the only means
To stop effusion of our Christian blood
And 'stablish quietness on every side.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
Well, my good lord; and as the only means
To stop effusion of our Christian blood
And 'stablish quietness on every side.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Ay, marry, uncle; for I always thought
It was both impious and unnatural
That such immanity and bloody strife
Should reign among professors of one faith.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Ay, marry, uncle; for I always thought
It was both impious and unnatural
That such immanity and bloody strife
Should reign among professors of one faith.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
Beside, my lord, the sooner to effect
And surer bind this knot of amity,
The Earl of Armagnac, near knit to Charles,
A man of great authority in France,
Proffers his only daughter to your grace
In marriage, with a large and sumptuous dowry.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Marriage, uncle! alas, my years are young!
And fitter is my study and my books
Than wanton dalliance with a paramour.
Yet call the ambassador; and, as you please,
So let them have their answers every one:
I shall be well content with any choice
Tends to God's glory and my country's weal.

Enter CARDINAL OF WINCHESTER in Cardinal's habit, a Legate and two Ambassadors

sentences.speaker: EXETER
sentences.text:
What! is my Lord of Winchester install'd,
And call'd unto a cardinal's degree?
Then I perceive that will be verified
Henry the Fifth did sometime prophesy,
'If once he come to be a cardinal,
He'll make his cap co-equal with the crown.'

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
My lords ambassadors, your several suits
Have been consider'd and debated on.
And therefore are we certainly resolved
To draw conditions of a friendly peace;
Which by my Lord of Winchester we mean
Shall be transported presently to France.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
My lords ambassadors, your several suits

And therefore are we certainly resolved

Shall be transported presently to France.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
And for the proffer of my lord your master,
I have inform'd his highness so at large
As liking of the lady's virtuous gifts,
Her beauty and the value of her dower,
He doth intend she shall be England's queen.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
In argument and proof of which contract,
Bear her this jewel, pledge of my affection.
And so, my lord protector, see them guarded
And safely brought to Dover; where inshipp'd
Commit them to the fortune of the sea.

sentences.speaker: WINCHESTER
sentences.text:
Stay, my lord legate: you shall first receive
The sum of money which I promised
Should be deliver'd to his holiness
For clothing me in these grave ornaments.

sentences.speaker: WINCHESTER
sentences.text:
Stay, my lord legate: you shall first receive
The sum of money which I promised
Should be deliver'd to his holiness
For clothing me in these grave ornaments.

sentences.speaker: LEGATE
sentences.text: I will attend upon your lordship's leisure.

sentences.speaker: WINCHESTER
sentences.text:
[Aside] Now Winchester will not submit, I trow,
Or be inferior to the proudest peer.
Humphrey of Gloucester, thou shalt well perceive
That, neither in birth or for authority,
The bishop will be overborne by thee:
I'll either make thee stoop and bend thy knee,
Or sack this country with a mutiny.

Enter CHARLES, BURGUNDY, ALENCON, BASTARD OF ORLEANS, REIGNIER, JOAN LA PUCELLE, and forces

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
These news, my lord, may cheer our drooping spirits:
'Tis said the stout Parisians do revolt
And turn again unto the warlike French.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
These news, my lord, may cheer our drooping spirits:
'Tis said the stout Parisians do revolt
And turn again unto the warlike French.

sentences.speaker: ALENCON
sentences.text:
Then march to Paris, royal Charles of France,
And keep not back your powers in dalliance.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
Peace be amongst them, if they turn to us;
Else, ruin combat with their palaces!

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
Peace be amongst them, if they turn to us;
Else, ruin combat with their palaces!
Enter Scout

sentences.speaker: SCOUT
sentences.text:
Success unto our valiant general,
And happiness to his accomplices!

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text: What tidings send our scouts? I prithee, speak.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text: What tidings send our scouts? I prithee, speak.

sentences.speaker: SCOUT
sentences.text:
The English army, that divided was
Into two parties, is now conjoined in one,
And means to give you battle presently.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
Somewhat too sudden, sirs, the warning is;
But we will presently provide for them.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
Somewhat too sudden, sirs, the warning is;
But we will presently provide for them.

sentences.speaker: BURGUNDY
sentences.text:
I trust the ghost of Talbot is not there:
Now he is gone, my lord, you need not fear.

sentences.speaker: BURGUNDY
sentences.text:
I trust the ghost of Talbot is not there:
Now he is gone, my lord, you need not fear.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
Of all base passions, fear is most accursed.
Command the conquest, Charles, it shall be thine,
Let Henry fret and all the world repine.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text: Then on, my lords; and France be fortunate!

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
The regent conquers, and the Frenchmen fly.
Now help, ye charming spells and periapts;
And ye choice spirits that admonish me
And give me signs of future accidents.

Thunder

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
You speedy helpers, that are substitutes
Under the lordly monarch of the north,
Appear and aid me in this enterprise.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
This speedy and quick appearance argues proof
Of your accustom'd diligence to me.
Now, ye familiar spirits, that are cull'd
Out of the powerful regions under earth,
Help me this once, that France may get the field.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:

Enter Fiends
They walk, and speak not

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
O, hold me not with silence over-long!
Where I was wont to feed you with my blood,
I'll lop a member off and give it you
In earnest of further benefit,
So you do condescend to help me now.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:

They hang their heads

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
No hope to have redress? My body shall
Pay recompense, if you will grant my suit.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
No hope to have redress? My body shall
Pay recompense, if you will grant my suit.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
Cannot my body nor blood-sacrifice
Entreat you to your wonted furtherance?
Then take my soul, my body, soul and all,
Before that England give the French the foil.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:

Entreat you to your wonted furtherance?
Then take my soul, my body, soul and all,
Before that England give the French the foil.
They depart

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
See, they forsake me! Now the time is come
That France must vail her lofty-plumed crest
And let her head fall into England's lap.
My ancient incantations are too weak,
And hell too strong for me to buckle with:
Now, France, thy glory droopeth to the dust.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Damsel of France, I think I have you fast:
Unchain your spirits now with spelling charms
And try if they can gain your liberty.
A goodly prize, fit for the devil's grace!
See, how the ugly wench doth bend her brows,
As if with Circe she would change my shape!

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text: Changed to a worser shape thou canst not be.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
O, Charles the Dauphin is a proper man;
No shape but his can please your dainty eye.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
A plaguing mischief light on Charles and thee!
And may ye both be suddenly surprised
By bloody hands, in sleeping on your beds!

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Fell banning hag, enchantress, hold thy tongue!

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text: I prithee, give me leave to curse awhile.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Curse, miscreant, when thou comest to the stake.
Exeunt
Alarum. Enter SUFFOLK with MARGARET in his hand

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: Be what thou wilt, thou art my prisoner.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: Be what thou wilt, thou art my prisoner.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
O fairest beauty, do not fear nor fly!
For I will touch thee but with reverent hands;
I kiss these fingers for eternal peace,
And lay them gently on thy tender side.
Who art thou? say, that I may honour thee.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Margaret my name, and daughter to a king,
The King of Naples, whosoe'er thou art.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Margaret my name, and daughter to a king,
The King of Naples, whosoe'er thou art.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
An earl I am, and Suffolk am I call'd.
Be not offended, nature's miracle,
Thou art allotted to be ta'en by me:
So doth the swan her downy cygnets save,
Keeping them prisoner underneath her wings.
Yet, if this servile usage once offend.
Go, and be free again, as Suffolk's friend.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
O, stay! I have no power to let her pass;
My hand would free her, but my heart says no
As plays the sun upon the glassy streams,
Twinkling another counterfeited beam,
So seems this gorgeous beauty to mine eyes.
Fain would I woo her, yet I dare not speak:
I'll call for pen and ink, and write my mind.
Fie, de la Pole! disable not thyself;
Hast not a tongue? is she not here?
Wilt thou be daunted at a woman's sight?
Ay, beauty's princely majesty is such,
Confounds the tongue and makes the senses rough.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Say, Earl of Suffolk—if thy name be so—
What ransom must I pay before I pass?
For I perceive I am thy prisoner.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Say, Earl of Suffolk—if thy name be so—
What ransom must I pay before I pass?
For I perceive I am thy prisoner.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
How canst thou tell she will deny thy suit,
Before thou make a trial of her love?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: Why speak'st thou not? what ransom must I pay?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: Why speak'st thou not? what ransom must I pay?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
She's beautiful, and therefore to be woo'd;
She is a woman, therefore to be won.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: Wilt thou accept of ransom? yea, or no.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: Wilt thou accept of ransom? yea, or no.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Fond man, remember that thou hast a wife;
Then how can Margaret be thy paramour?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Fond man, remember that thou hast a wife;
Then how can Margaret be thy paramour?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: I were best to leave him, for he will not hear.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: There all is marr'd; there lies a cooling card.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: He talks at random; sure, the man is mad.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: He talks at random; sure, the man is mad.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: And yet a dispensation may be had.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: And yet I would that you would answer me.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
I'll win this Lady Margaret. For whom?
Why, for my king: tush, that's a wooden thing!

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:

Why, for my king: tush, that's a wooden thing!

sentences.speaker: MARGARET

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Yet so my fancy may be satisfied,
And peace established between these realms
But there remains a scruple in that too;
For though her father be the King of Naples,
Duke of Anjou and Maine, yet is he poor,
And our nobility will scorn the match.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: Hear ye, captain, are you not at leisure?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: Hear ye, captain, are you not at leisure?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
It shall be so, disdain they ne'er so much.
Henry is youthful and will quickly yield.
Madam, I have a secret to reveal.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK

It shall be so, disdain they ne'er so much.
Henry is youthful and will quickly yield.
Madam, I have a secret to reveal.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
What though I be enthrall'd? he seems a knight,
And will not any way dishonour me.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
What though I be enthrall'd? he seems a knight,
And will not any way dishonour me.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: Lady, vouchsafe to listen what I say.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Perhaps I shall be rescued by the French;
And then I need not crave his courtesy.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: Sweet madam, give me a hearing in a cause—

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: Tush, women have been captivate ere now.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: Lady, wherefore talk you so?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: I cry you mercy, 'tis but Quid for Quo.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Say, gentle princess, would you not suppose
Your bondage happy, to be made a queen?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
MARGARET
SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
And so shall you,
If happy England's royal king be free.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: Why, what concerns his freedom unto me?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: Why, what concerns his freedom unto me?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
I'll undertake to make thee Henry's queen,
To put a golden sceptre in thy hand
And set a precious crown upon thy head,
If thou wilt condescend to be my—

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: What?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: His love.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: I am unworthy to be Henry's wife.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
No, gentle madam; I unworthy am
To woo so fair a dame to be his wife,
And have no portion in the choice myself.
How say you, madam, are ye so content?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:

And have no portion in the choice myself.
How say you, madam, are ye so content?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK

How say you, madam, are ye so content?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: An if my father please, I am content.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Then call our captains and our colours forth.
And, madam, at your father's castle walls
We'll crave a parley, to confer with him.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK

We'll crave a parley, to confer with him.
A parley sounded. Enter REIGNIER on the walls
A parley sounded. Enter REIGNIER on the walls

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: See, Reignier, see, thy daughter prisoner!

sentences.speaker: REIGNIER
sentences.text: To whom?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: To me.

sentences.speaker: REIGNIER
sentences.text:
Suffolk, what remedy?
I am a soldier, and unapt to weep,
Or to exclaim on fortune's fickleness.

sentences.speaker: REIGNIER
sentences.text:
Suffolk, what remedy?
I am a soldier, and unapt to weep,
Or to exclaim on fortune's fickleness.

sentences.speaker: SU FFOLK
SU FFOLK
SU FFOLK
REIGNIER
sentences.text: Speaks Suffolk as he thinks?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Fair Margaret knows
That Suffolk doth not flatter, face, or feign.

sentences.speaker: REIGNIER
sentences.text:
Upon thy princely warrant, I descend
To give thee answer of thy just demand.
Exit from the walls

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: And here I will expect thy coming.
Trumpets sound. Enter REIGNIER, below
Trumpets sound. Enter REIGNIER, below

sentences.speaker: REIGNIER
sentences.text:
Welcome, brave earl, into our territories:
Command in Anjou what your honour pleases.

sentences.speaker: REIGNIER
sentences.text:
Welcome, brave earl, into our territories:
Command in Anjou what your honour pleases.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Thanks, Reignier, happy for so sweet a child,
Fit to be made companion with a king:
What answer makes your grace unto my suit?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Thanks, Reignier, happy for so sweet a child,
Fit to be made companion with a king:
What answer makes your grace unto my suit?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Thanks, Reignier, happy for so sweet a child,
Fit to be made companion with a king:
What answer makes your grace unto my suit?

sentences.speaker: REIGNIER
sentences.text:
Since thou dost deign to woo her little worth
To be the princely bride of such a lord;
Upon condition I may quietly
Enjoy mine own, the country Maine and Anjou,
Free from oppression or the stroke of war,
My daughter shall be Henry's, if he please.

sentences.speaker: REIGNIER
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
SUFFOLK
SUFFOLK
REIGNIER
sentences.text:
And I again, in Henry's royal name,
As deputy unto that gracious king,
Give thee her hand, for sign of plighted faith.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Reignier of France, I give thee kingly thanks,
Because this is in traffic of a king.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Reignier of France, I give thee kingly thanks,
Because this is in traffic of a king.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Reignier of France, I give thee kingly thanks,
Because this is in traffic of a king.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: Reignier of France, I thank you in the name of my king, because this is the king's business.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: Reignier of France, I thank you in the name of my king, because this is the king's business.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: Reignier of France, I thank you in the name of my king, because this is the king's business.

sentences.speaker: REIGNIER
sentences.text:
I do embrace thee, as I would embrace
The Christian prince, King Henry, were he here.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Farewell, my lord: good wishes, praise and prayers
Shall Suffolk ever have of Margaret.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:

Shall Suffolk ever have of Margaret.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:

Shall Suffolk ever have of Margaret.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Farewell, sweet madam: but hark you, Margaret;
No princely commendations to my king?

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Farewell, sweet madam: but hark you, Margaret;
No princely commendations to my king?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Such commendations as becomes a maid,
A virgin and his servant, say to him.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Such commendations as becomes a maid,
A virgin and his servant, say to him.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
SUFFOLK
MARGARET
sentences.text:
Yes, my good lord, a pure unspotted heart,
Never yet taint with love, I send the king.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: And this withal.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text: And this withal.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
That for thyself: I will not so presume
To send such peevish tokens to a king.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
That for thyself: I will not so presume

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Bring forth that sorceress condemn'd to burn.
Enter JOAN LA PUCELLE, guarded, and a Shepherd

sentences.speaker: SHEPHERD
sentences.text:
Ah, Joan, this kills thy father's heart outright!
Have I sought every country far and near,
And, now it is my chance to find thee out,
Must I behold thy timeless cruel death?
Ah, Joan, sweet daughter Joan, I'll die with thee!

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
Decrepit miser! base ignoble wretch!
I am descended of a gentler blood:
Thou art no father nor no friend of mine.

sentences.speaker: SHEPHERD
sentences.text:
Out, out! My lords, an please you, 'tis not so;
I did beget her, all the parish knows:
Her mother liveth yet, can testify
She was the first fruit of my bachelorship.

Out, out! My lords, an please you, 'tis not so;
I did beget her, all the parish knows:
Her mother liveth yet, can testify
She was the first fruit of my bachelorship.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
This argues what her kind of life hath been,
Wicked and vile; and so her death concludes.

sentences.speaker: SHEPHERD
sentences.text:
Fie, Joan, that thou wilt be so obstacle!
God knows thou art a collop of my flesh;
And for thy sake have I shed many a tear:
Deny me not, I prithee, gentle Joan.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
Peasant, avaunt! You have suborn'd this man,
Of purpose to obscure my noble birth.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
Peasant, avaunt! You have suborn'd this man,
Of purpose to obscure my noble birth.

sentences.speaker: SHEPHERD
sentences.text:
'Tis true, I gave a noble to the priest
The morn that I was wedded to her mother.
Kneel down and take my blessing, good my girl.
Wilt thou not stoop? Now cursed be the time
Of thy nativity! I would the milk
Thy mother gave thee when thou suck'dst her breast,
Had been a little ratsbane for thy sake!
Or else, when thou didst keep my lambs a-field,
I wish some ravenous wolf had eaten thee!
Dost thou deny thy father, cursed drab?
O, burn her, burn her! hanging is too good.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Take her away; for she hath lived too long,
To fill the world with vicious qualities.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
First, let me tell you whom you have condemn'd:
Not me begotten of a shepherd swain,
But issued from the progeny of kings;
Virtuous and holy; chosen from above,
By inspiration of celestial grace,
To work exceeding miracles on earth.
I never had to do with wicked spirits:
But you, that are polluted with your lusts,
Stain'd with the guiltless blood of innocents,
Corrupt and tainted with a thousand vices,
Because you want the grace that others have,
You judge it straight a thing impossible
To compass wonders but by help of devils.
No, misconceived! Joan of Arc hath been
A virgin from her tender infancy,
Chaste and immaculate in very thought;
Whose maiden blood, thus rigorously effused,
Will cry for vengeance at the gates of heaven.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text: Ay, ay: away with her to execution!

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
And hark ye, sirs; because she is a maid,
Spare for no faggots, let there be enow:
Place barrels of pitch upon the fatal stake,
That so her torture may be shortened.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
Will nothing turn your unrelenting hearts?
Then, Joan, discover thine infirmity,
That warranteth by law to be thy privilege.
I am with child, ye bloody homicides:
Murder not then the fruit within my womb,
Although ye hale me to a violent death.

Is all your strict preciseness come to this?

sentences.speaker: YORK

She and the Dauphin have been juggling:
I did imagine what would be her refuge.

sentences.speaker: YORK

She and the Dauphin have been juggling:
I did imagine what would be her refuge.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
Well, go to; we'll have no bastards live;
Especially since Charles must father it.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
You are deceived; my child is none of his:
It was Alencon that enjoy'd my love.

sentences.speaker: YORK

Alencon! that notorious Machiavel!
It dies, an if it had a thousand lives.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE

O, give me leave, I have deluded you:

But Reignier, king of Naples, that prevail'd.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK

sentences.speaker: WARWICK

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Why, here's a girl! I think she knows not well,
There were so many, whom she may accuse.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: It's sign she hath been liberal and free.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
And yet, forsooth, she is a virgin pure.
Strumpet, thy words condemn thy brat and thee:
Use no entreaty, for it is in vain.

sentences.speaker: JOAN LA PUCELLE
sentences.text:
Then lead me hence; with whom I leave my curse:
May never glorious sun reflex his beams
Upon the country where you make abode;
But darkness and the gloomy shade of death
Environ you, till mischief and despair
Drive you to break your necks or hang yourselves!

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Break thou in pieces and consume to ashes,
Thou foul accursed minister of hell!
Enter CARDINAL OF WINCHESTER, attended

sentences.speaker: WINCHESTER
sentences.text:
Lord regent, I do greet your excellence
With letters of commission from the king.
For know, my lords, the states of Christendom,
Moved with remorse of these outrageous broils,
Have earnestly implored a general peace
Betwixt our nation and the aspiring French;
And here at hand the Dauphin and his train
Approacheth, to confer about some matter.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Is all our travail turn'd to this effect?
After the slaughter of so many peers,
So many captains, gentlemen and soldiers,
That in this quarrel have been overthrown
And sold their bodies for their country's benefit,
Shall we at last conclude effeminate peace?
Have we not lost most part of all the towns,
By treason, falsehood and by treachery,
Our great progenitors had conquered?
O Warwick, Warwick! I foresee with grief
The utter loss of all the realm of France.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
Be patient, York: if we conclude a peace,
It shall be with such strict and severe covenants
As little shall the Frenchmen gain thereby.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
Be patient, York: if we conclude a peace,
It shall be with such strict and severe covenants
As little shall the Frenchmen gain thereby.
Enter CHARLES, ALENCON, BASTARD OF ORLEANS, REIGNIER, and others

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
Since, lords of England, it is thus agreed
That peaceful truce shall be proclaim'd in France,
We come to be informed by yourselves
What the conditions of that league must be.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Speak, Winchester; for boiling choler chokes
The hollow passage of my poison'd voice,
By sight of these our baleful enemies.

sentences.speaker: WINCHESTER
sentences.text:
Charles, and the rest, it is enacted thus:
That, in regard King Henry gives consent,
Of mere compassion and of lenity,
To ease your country of distressful war,
And suffer you to breathe in fruitful peace,
You shall become true liegemen to his crown:
And Charles, upon condition thou wilt swear
To pay him tribute, submit thyself,
Thou shalt be placed as viceroy under him,
And still enjoy thy regal dignity.

sentences.speaker: WINCHESTER
sentences.text:

You shall become true liegemen to his crown:
And Charles, upon condition thou wilt swear
To pay him tribute, submit thyself,
Thou shalt be placed as viceroy under him,
And still enjoy thy regal dignity.

sentences.speaker: ALENCON
sentences.text:
Must he be then as shadow of himself?
Adorn his temples with a coronet,
And yet, in substance and authority,
Retain but privilege of a private man?
This proffer is absurd and reasonless.

sentences.speaker: ALENCON
sentences.text:

And yet, in substance and authority,

This proffer is absurd and reasonless.

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
sentences.text:
'Tis known already that I am possess'd
With more than half the Gallian territories,
And therein reverenced for their lawful king:
Shall I, for lucre of the rest unvanquish'd,
Detract so much from that prerogative,
As to be call'd but viceroy of the whole?
No, lord ambassador, I'll rather keep
That which I have than, coveting for more,
Be cast from possibility of all.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Insulting Charles! hast thou by secret means
Used intercession to obtain a league,
And, now the matter grows to compromise,
Stand'st thou aloof upon comparison?
Either accept the title thou usurp'st,
Of benefit proceeding from our king
And not of any challenge of desert,
Or we will plague thee with incessant wars.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:
Insulting Charles! hast thou by secret means
Used intercession to obtain a league,
And, now the matter grows to compromise,
Stand'st thou aloof upon comparison?
Either accept the title thou usurp'st,
Of benefit proceeding from our king
And not of any challenge of desert,
Or we will plague thee with incessant wars.

sentences.speaker: YORK
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: REIGNIER
sentences.text:
My lord, you do not well in obstinacy
To cavil in the course of this contract:
If once it be neglected, ten to one
We shall not find like opportunity.

sentences.speaker: REIGNIER
sentences.text:
My lord, you do not well in obstinacy
To cavil in the course of this contract:
If once it be neglected, ten to one
We shall not find like opportunity.

sentences.speaker: ALENCON
sentences.text:
To say the truth, it is your policy
To save your subjects from such massacre
And ruthless slaughters as are daily seen
By our proceeding in hostility;
And therefore take this compact of a truce,
Although you break it when your pleasure serves.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: How say'st thou, Charles? shall our condition stand?

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: How say'st thou, Charles? shall our condition stand?

sentences.speaker: CHARLES
YORK
sentences.text:
Then swear allegiance to his majesty,
As thou art knight, never to disobey
Nor be rebellious to the crown of England,
Thou, nor thy nobles, to the crown of England.
So, now dismiss your army when ye please:
Hang up your ensign, let your drums be still,
For here we entertain a solemn peace.

Enter SUFFOLK in conference with KING HENRY VI, GLOUCESTER and EXETER

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Your wondrous rare description, noble earl,
Of beauteous Margaret hath astonish'd me:
Her virtues graced with external gifts
Do breed love's settled passions in my heart:
And like as rigor of tempestuous gusts
Provokes the mightiest hulk against the tide,
So am I driven by breath of her renown
Either to suffer shipwreck or arrive
Where I may have fruition of her love.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Tush, my good lord, this superficial tale
Is but a preface of her worthy praise;
The chief perfections of that lovely dame
Had I sufficient skill to utter them,
Would make a volume of enticing lines,
Able to ravish any dull conceit:
And, which is more, she is not so divine,
So full-replete with choice of all delights,
But with as humble lowliness of mind
She is content to be at your command;
Command, I mean, of virtuous chaste intents,
To love and honour Henry as her lord.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
And otherwise will Henry ne'er presume.
Therefore, my lord protector, give consent
That Margaret may be England's royal queen.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
So should I give consent to flatter sin.
You know, my lord, your highness is betroth'd
Unto another lady of esteem:
How shall we then dispense with that contract,
And not deface your honour with reproach?

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
So should I give consent to flatter sin.
You know, my lord, your highness is betroth'd
Unto another lady of esteem:
How shall we then dispense with that contract,
And not deface your honour with reproach?

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
So should I give consent to flatter sin.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
As doth a ruler with unlawful oaths;
Or one that, at a triumph having vow'd
To try his strength, forsaketh yet the lists
By reason of his adversary's odds:
A poor earl's daughter is unequal odds,
And therefore may be broke without offence.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
As doth a ruler with unlawful oaths;
Or one that, at a triumph having vow'd
To try his strength, forsaketh yet the lists
By reason of his adversary's odds:
A poor earl's daughter is unequal odds,
And therefore may be broke without offence.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
Why, what, I pray, is Margaret more than that?
Her father is no better than an earl,
Although in glorious titles he excel.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
Why, what, I pray, is Margaret more than that?
Her father is no better than an earl,
Although in glorious titles he excel.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Yes, lord, her father is a king,
The King of Naples and Jerusalem;
And of such great authority in France
As his alliance will confirm our peace
And keep the Frenchmen in allegiance.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Yes, lord, her father is a king,
The King of Naples and Jerusalem;
And of such great authority in France
As his alliance will confirm our peace
And keep the Frenchmen in allegiance.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Yes, lord, her father is a king,
The King of Naples and Jerusalem;
And of such great authority in France
As his alliance will confirm our peace
And keep the Frenchmen in allegiance.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text:
And so the Earl of Armagnac may do,
Because he is near kinsman unto Charles.

sentences.speaker: EXETER
sentences.text:
Beside, his wealth doth warrant a liberal dower,
Where Reignier sooner will receive than give.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
A dower, my lords! disgrace not so your king,
That he should be so abject, base and poor,
To choose for wealth and not for perfect love.
Henry is able to enrich his queen
And not seek a queen to make him rich:
So worthless peasants bargain for their wives,
As market-men for oxen, sheep, or horse.
Marriage is a matter of more worth
Than to be dealt in by attorneyship;
Not whom we will, but whom his grace affects,
Must be companion of his nuptial bed:
And therefore, lords, since he affects her most,
It most of all these reasons bindeth us,
In our opinions she should be preferr'd.
For what is wedlock forced but a hell,
An age of discord and continual strife?
Whereas the contrary bringeth bliss,
And is a pattern of celestial peace.
Whom should we match with Henry, being a king,
But Margaret, that is daughter to a king?
Her peerless feature, joined with her birth,
Approves her fit for none but for a king:
Her valiant courage and undaunted spirit,
More than in women commonly is seen,
Will answer our hope in issue of a king;
For Henry, son unto a conqueror,
Is likely to beget more conquerors,
If with a lady of so high resolve
As is fair Margaret he be link'd in love.
Then yield, my lords; and here conclude with me
That Margaret shall be queen, and none but she.

Will answer our hope in issue of a king;
For Henry, son unto a conqueror,
Is likely to beget more conquerors,
If with a lady of so high resolve
As is fair Margaret he be link'd in love.
Then yield, my lords; and here conclude with me
That Margaret shall be queen, and none but she.

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
A dower, my lords! disgrace not so your king,
That he should be so abject, base and poor,
To choose for wealth and not for perfect love.
Henry is able to enrich his queen
And not seek a queen to make him rich:
So worthless peasants bargain for their wives,
As market-men for oxen, sheep, or horse.
Marriage is a matter of more worth
Than to be dealt in by attorneyship;
Not whom we will, but whom his grace affects,
Must be companion of his nuptial bed:
And therefore, lords, since he affects her most,
It most of all these reasons bindeth us,
In our opinions she should be preferr'd.
For what is wedlock forced but a hell,
An age of discord and continual strife?
Whereas the contrary bringeth bliss,
And is a pattern of celestial peace.
Whom should we match with Henry, being a king,
But Margaret, that is daughter to a king?
Her peerless feature, joined with her birth,
Approves her fit for none but for a king:
Her valiant courage and undaunted spirit,
More than in women commonly is seen,
Will answer our hope in issue of a king;
For Henry, son unto a conqueror,
Is likely to beget more conquerors,
If with a lady of so high resolve
As is fair Margaret he be link'd in love.
Then yield, my lords; and here conclude with me
That Margaret shall be queen, and none but she.

sentences.speaker: KING HENRY VI
sentences.text:
Whether it be through force of your report,
My noble Lord of Suffolk, or for that
My tender youth was never yet attaint
With any passion of inflaming love,
I cannot tell; but this I am assured,
I feel such sharp dissension in my breast,
Such fierce alarums both of hope and fear,
As I am sick with working of my thoughts.
Take, therefore, shipping; post, my lord, to France;
Agree to any covenants, and procure
That Lady Margaret do vouchsafe to come
To cross the seas to England and be crown'd
King Henry's faithful and anointed queen:
For your expenses and sufficient charge,
Among the people gather up a tenth.
Be gone, I say; for, till you do return,
I rest perplexed with a thousand cares.
And you, good uncle, banish all offence:
If you do censure me by what you were,
Not what you are, I know it will excuse
This sudden execution of my will.
And so, conduct me where, from company,
I may revolve and ruminate my grief.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
sentences.text: Ay, grief, I fear me, both at first and last.
Exeunt GLOUCESTER and EXETER

sentences.speaker: SUFFOLK
sentences.text:
Thus Suffolk hath prevail'd; and thus he goes,
As did the youthful Paris once to Greece,
With hope to find the like event in love,
But prosper better than the Trojan did.
Margaret shall now be queen, and rule the king;
But I will rule both her, the king and realm.
